summary: DHH argues that earning attention through genuine sharing and teaching is far more valuable than buying it through advertising. He rejects the term 'content marketing' as too contrived and transactional, insisting the best results come from authentically sharing what you're doing and learning. He points out that most businesses don't have secret recipes—they succeed on execution and timing—so openly sharing knowledge costs nothing. The deep connection formed when you genuinely teach someone or change their thinking creates long-lasting relationships that eventually convert to customers, even if the timeline is unmeasurable.
key_points: Most businesses have no secret recipe worth protecting; they succeed or fail on execution and timing
The term 'content marketing' implies a contrived, transactional approach that undermines authenticity
Earning attention through genuine teaching creates far deeper connections than paid advertising
The ROI of authentic sharing is real but often unquantifiable—people may follow for years before becoming customers
Sharing knowledge should be intrinsically rewarding, not contingent on measurable returns
money_quotes: Most businesses are not fucking Coca-Cola. They don't have this secret recipe that's the foundation of their success.
The vast majority of businesses succeed or fail on the basis of their execution and their timing.
I absolutely loathe the term content marketing because it sounds so contrived.
The good stuff comes out when you're simply sharing what you're doing without this transactional approach to it.

The connection you establish when you truly teach someone something, when you truly change their mind on something, or at least provoke them to think about that topic in a new way, it's just so much deeper.
Oh man, I read all your stuff for years. And then, hey, I just signed up for Basecamp yesterday. How do you even track or quantify that?
Just you put good stuff into the universe and most of the time the universe will return good stuff to you.
key_insight: Authentic sharing and teaching builds deeper, more valuable customer relationships than any advertising spend, even if the returns are impossible to quantify.
